def: s.m. Head, skull, pate; top, highest part or point, summit, crown, pinnacle; beginning, commencement; — adv. At the beginning
subs: To be crushed in the commencement of one's undertaking
To walk with the head on high; to walk on tip-toe; to walk conceitedly, to strut, to look big (syn. akaṛnā)

v.n. To raise the head; to rise up; to look up; to exalt oneself; to rise (against), to make head (against), to rebel, mutiny, revolt; to be refractory or contumacious; to be contentious or quarrelsome
(-ke), To get into the head (of), to take possession (of one, — an evil spirit, &c.)
adv. With all (one's) heart and soul, most willingly, most cordially or cheerfully
To tie up the hair, to plait or braid the hair
s.m. One who carries a load or bundle on his head
'The head to be heavy,' to have a headache; to be giddy
To dash the head (against the ground); &c. ( = sir mārnā), q.v.
adv. On the head (of); close at hand
To raise (a house, &c., with noise), to cause a tumult or confusion, to make a great noise
v.t. To make much of, to show respect to; to spoil (a child, &c.); to raise an inferior above oneself

(-ke), To be spoilt by kindness or indulgence, to impose on (one's) goodness or kindness; to behave with extreme rudeness or incivility (to), to insult, treat with indignity; — to be possessed (by an evil spirit)
(-ke), To place a thatched roof on the head (of), to lay a great burden (on), to impose (on); to lay (one) under an obligation
(-ke), 'To throw ashes or dust on the head,' to mourn, lament
To have a stamping or noise going on over head; to be distracted by noise; to be burdened or bowed down (by difficulties, &c.)
v.t. To place on the head; to treat with great respect, to honour, revere
(colloq.), lit. 'To place a black pot on the head'; to be disgraced, be defamed; to become shameless
(-ke), To place the hand on the head (of); to take under (one's) protection or care, to foster, to patronize
(-ke), To fall on the head (of), to devolve (on)
(-kā), To hold the head (of); to comfort, console; to help
s.f. 'Head-breaking'; wrangling, fighting; — affectation of civility with internal hatred
To turn or move about the head in arduous exertion, to labour or strive in vain
v.n. 'Head to be broken'; a great fight to occur; — to have a splitting headache; — to have the menses
(-kā), To break the head (of), crack or split the skull (of); to wrangle, quarrel, fight; — to rack (one's own) brains, to labour or strive in vain
s.f. A splitting headache; — brain-racking work, absurd or vain labour
(-se), To turn away the head or face (from), to prove refractory, to revolt, rebel
To beat the head (in trouble or anguish), to mourn, lament, regret
v.t. To take upon oneself, to be responsible (for); to risk (one's) life (for)
v.t. lit. 'To break the head and take,' to take (a thing) by force or violence; to extort (money, &c.) by threatening to torture oneself
(-kā), To break the head (of = sir phoṛnā, q.v.); to subdue
(apnā), To weary (one's) brain, to bother or trouble ooneself
adj. lit. 'Heads-joined'; (in Bot.) connate; — sir joṛnā, or sir-joṛke baiṭhnā, To lay (their) heads together, to hold a council or meeting; to plot together; — to live in peace
s.m. lit. 'Having a bushy head and a monstrous mouth'; an ogre, a fiend
To bend down the head; to bow, nod; to be obedfent or submissive, to submit; to be humbled or humiliated
v.t. To lay on the head (of another, -ke)
adj. Spoilt (by indulgence, &c.); proud, haughty, arrogant, insolent, beyond control; rude, saucy

v.t. 'To place on the head'; to make much of; to pet or spoil (a child, &c.); to exalt, honour, revere, reverence; — to offer oneself as a sacrifice, to devote ooneself; — to hold (one's, apnā) head high, to assume, to be arrogant
To talk under the influence (of an evil spirit, &c.)
To die laying the guilt of one's blood at the door (of another)

(-ke, or mere), 'To mount on the head' (of); to take advantage (of), impose upon, take liberties (with), to be rude or insolent (to); to tyrannize (over); — to be placed or debited to the account (of); to be attributed or imputed (to)
adj. (colloq.) lit. 'Head-hurting'; tedious, wearisome; excessive, burdensome; hard, difficult; — adv. At once, immediately
(-kā), To split the skull (of); — to insist on having one's own way
or sir-dharū, s.m. Head of a village, quarter, or family, &c.; patron, master
'To place (one's) head low,' to be submissive or obedient
or sir ḍulānā (apnā), To shake or beat one's head (from affliction, &c.), to mourn ( = sir pīṭnā, q.v.)
s.m. Deep water above a man's head; — adj. Enough to drown; — perfectly wet
(-kā), To be deflowered, to be violated
(-kā), 'To colour the head,' to make the head bleed
(-ke). To apply (oneself) steadily (to), to persevere; to follow persistently or doggedly (after)
lit 'The turban for the head'; the conducting of an affair depends on the leader or head; followers fare as their chief
'To wrap the winding-sheet round the head'; to engage in a desperate undertaking, to hold one's life in one's hand
adj. & s.m. Rebellious, mutinous, turbulent; — a rebellious or turbulent fellow
To be the head or leader (of), to have the badge of honour or distinction
adj. & s.m. (f. -ī), Beheaded, decapitated, headless; — a decapitated or headless man
v.t. To lay on the shoulders (of, -ke), to make liable; — to set by the ears; — v.n. To do the hair
To turn (on or upon), to attack
To go quickly but with respect
(-kā), 'To devour one's head'; to worry, pester, tease; — to make a great noise
adj. & s.m. 'Head-destroying,' risking the head or life, intrepid, bold, fearless, resolute (soldier), venturesome, adventurous; — one who risks his life, a fearless or intrepid or resolute man
'To destroy the head,' to put one's head or life at stake; to trouble or bother (one's) head
s.f. Placing the head at stake, venturesomeness, intrepidity, daring, resoluteness; perseverance, enterprise
To scratch the head; — to court punishment
adv. On the head, head-foremost, headlong, amain
adv. With all (one's) might; impetuously; head-foremost, headlong
s.f. Comb (of a cock), crest (of a bird)
s.f. The Hindū marriage ceremony of braiding a bride's hair for the first time
(or ghuṭnoṅ-meṅ) denā, To place the head on the feet or knees, to be dejected or downcast
The head to be in a whirl, to suffer from vertigo; the head to be turned, to be insane
(-ke, or mere), To be accused (of), be charged (with); — to suffer hardship
To beat the head; to rack the brains, to make strenuous efforts, to try hard, take great pains; to make diligent search
s.m. A gem or jewel worn in a crest or upon the head
adj. & s.m. Having the head shaven; — one whose head is shaved, a shaveling; — a shaven crown
(-kā, or apnā), To have or get the head shaved; — to adopt the life of a mendicant or devotee
(-kā), To shave the head (of); to cozen, cheat, fleece
v.n. (colloq.) To have hair on the head; to be able to endure or suffer
v.t. To get or receive it (viz. a blow) on the head
To bend or bow the head, make obeisance, &c. ( = sir-jhukānā)
adv. Neither head nor foot (to it), without foundation, groundless
sir nīćā karnā, To lower the head; to be dejected; to be abashed or ashamed; to be humbled or humiliated (syn. sir jhukānā)
s.m. The sign Anusvāra, or ćandra-vindu

(kisī-ke), To be constantly at (one), to worry, pester, harass, persecute; to pursue, hunt; to stick close (to a person, or thing, or work); to fall foul (of), pick a quarrel (with); to accuse falsely; to insist (on), to persist; to be ready or eager (for); to get hold (of), come (by), grasp, pocket
(or lākh) sir-kā, adj. (colloq.), lit. 'Having a hundred (or many) heads'; headstrong, obstinate; — possessing the brains or reason of a hundred heads
To be menstruant, to have the menses.
wety: Borrowed from Classical Persian شر (šarr), from Arabic شَرّ (šarr).

def: s.f. Madness, insanity; raving, drivelling talk
subs: adj. & s.m. (f. -ī), Mad, crazy; — a madman, a crazy person
or siṛ lagnā (-ko), To go off one's head, to become mad, to rave.

def: s.f. Damp, &c. ( = sīl, q.v.). s.f. Rock, stone; a slab, flag-stone; a flat stone or slab on which condiments, &c. are ground with a muller; — threshold, door-frame
subs: s.m. The slab and muller with which condiments, spices, &c. are ground
and silā-jīt, corr. salā-jīt (S. śilā-jatu, and śilā-jit), s.m. Storax; stone-lac; red chalk; bitumen; benzoin; mineral coal; a beautiful crystallized foliated gypsum
s.m. lit. 'Rock-exudation'; storax, &c. ( = silā-jit); olibanum; incense; oil of cardamom
wety: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَیر (sayr), from Arabic سَيْر (sayr).

def: s.m. A plough; — the sun; — (in Hindī) s.m.f. Husbandry, agriculture; land under the immediate cultivation of the proprietor; first-rate land; — adv. Together, in partnership, jointly
wety: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَیر (sayr), from Arabic سَيْر (sayr).

def: s.m. Garlic; — water-mint.
wety: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَیر (sayr), from Arabic سَيْر (sayr).

def: s.m. The head, &c. ( = sir, and śiras, qq.v.); — the root of the pepper plant
subs: s.m. The head and feet; — śir-o-pāṅw denā, To place anyone's foot on another's head or neck (a form of salutation to betoken submissiveness).
wety: Borrowed from Classical Persian شر (šarr), from Arabic شَرّ (šarr).

ety: prob. fr. S. शिरा; but cf. sīr, sīl
def: s.m. A channel through which fields are watered; — an inundated field; moist land.
s.m. Head, top, point, end; extremity, tip; beginning; head-piece or foot-piece (of a bed, &c.)
subs: adv. From the end; from the beginning, ab initio, from the very first; first and foremost.

def: adj. & s.m. (f. -ī), Mad, insane, crazy; — a madman.
s.f. Any tubular vessel of the body, a nerve, tendon, fibre, gut, vein, blood-vessel, artery.
adj. Cool, moist, damp, raw; — s.m. A kind of sweetmeat made of meal and sugar (syn. ḥalwā; mohan-bhog)

ety: prob. fr. S. शिरा; but cf. sīr, sīl
def: s.m. A channel through which fields are watered; — an inundated field; moist land.
s.f. Any tubular vessel of the body, an artery, vein, nerve; — a channel (for irrigation); — a bucket, baling vessel; — flooding (a field)
subs: s.m. Network of vessels or veins; enlargement of the vessels of the eye
(˚rā+ut˚), s.m. Disease or morbid affection of the veins, &c.; redness and inflammation of the eyes.
